title: Felted wool cord
subtitle: Rolling merino roving into a round wet-felted cord, from a quick kitchen-counter method to a polished dense cord
excerpt: Roll a length of merino roving into a round wet-felted cord using either the quick kitchen-counter method for a soft, slightly irregular cord, or the extended polished method for a firm, smooth surface. Felted cords are used in vessel handles, jewellery, and sculptural accents. The method relies on consistent rolling pressure and the correct soap-to-water ratio to produce an even diameter.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the wet-felted cord-making tradition common to basket and jewellery making workshops. The kitchen-counter rolling method and the extended polished method are both standard guild and studio techniques; this description is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for felting. For cord-making, the roving is used in a continuous length rather than pulled into wisps; the ribbon rolls around itself under the pressure of the palms.
The vigorous agitation phase of wet felting. For cord-making, fulling is the rolling phase that compacts the core and firms and smooths the surface, shrinking the cord to its finished diameter.

content.text: A wet-felted cord is made by rolling a length of merino

between the palms or against a work surface until the fibres lock into a round, stable cord. The quick kitchen-counter method produces a cord of 8 to 12 mm diameter in 10 to 15 minutes. It is slightly irregular and soft, which suits decorative use. The extended polished method uses a tea towel as a rolling surface and continues
until the cord is dense and smooth, giving a firmer result suited to handles and jewellery. Both methods start with the same preparation. The difference is time and rolling intensity.

items.name: Merino roving (any colour)
items.qty: 20 to 40 g per 30 to 50 cm of cord

items.name: Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)

content.text: Step 1: Prepare the roving length. Fold the roving length over itself two to three times to produce a bundle that is one-quarter to one-third of the intended cord length. This folded bundle will be the core of the cord. Keep the ends tucked in if possible. A bundle of 15 g makes a cord of approximately 30 cm at 10 mm diameter. Adjust weight proportionally for longer or finer cords.

Step 2: Wet the bundle. Dissolve a small amount of olive-oil soap in hot water. Submerge the roving bundle and press down gently to saturate it fully. The bundle should be thoroughly wet but not wrung out. Cup the wet bundle in both hands and press gently into a rough cylinder shape; the soapy water helps the outer fibres begin to orient along the length.

Step 3: Quick kitchen-counter method. Place the wet bundle on a smooth, clean work surface. Place both palms flat on top of it and roll the bundle back and forth, applying moderate downward pressure, working from the centre toward each end. The bundle will elongate and begin to compact into a cylindrical form. Add more hot soapy water as the surface dries out. Continue rolling for 10 to 15 minutes, turning the cord by 90 degrees every few minutes so it rolls evenly on all sides. Stop when the cord holds its shape when lifted, feels firm to a gentle squeeze, and has a reasonably consistent diameter. The surface will have some texture and slight irregularity; this is characteristic of the quick method.

Step 4: Extended polished method. Once the cord is at the stage described in Step 3, continue working for a polished, denser result. Lay the cord on a cotton tea towel. Fold one half of the tea towel over the cord and roll it firmly back and forth on the work surface with both palms, applying firm downward pressure. The cloth provides additional friction and helps compact the surface fibres more than bare-hand rolling. Continue for 15 to 20 minutes further, rewetting with hot soapy water as needed and rotating the cord regularly. The cord is finished when the surface feels smooth and hard and resists compression between the thumb and forefinger. The diameter will be 20 to 30 per cent smaller than after the quick method.

Step 5: Finish the ends. Once the cord is at the intended density, tidy the ends. If the ends are frayed, fold the loose fibres back along the cord body and work them in by rolling the end specifically between thumb and forefinger with hot soapy water. If you want the ends cut clean for attachment, wait until the cord is dry then cut with sharp scissors. A cut end on dense cord does not unravel.

Step 6: Rinse and dry. Rinse the cord thoroughly in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge into cold water for 20 to 30 seconds. The cold shock closes the fibre scales and firms the surface further. Press between dry towels to extract water. Lay the cord straight on a dry flat surface to dry; if it dries in a curve it will hold that curve permanently. Once dry, the cord is ready to use or attach.

items.problem: The cord is lumpy with thick and thin sections
items.cause: The initial bundle was folded unevenly or the rolling pressure was concentrated in the middle
items.fix: Work each section of the cord individually: identify the thin spot, add a small wisp of dry roving at that point, wet it, and roll that section specifically. Concentrating the rolling at thick spots allows thin spots to catch up.

items.problem: The cord is oval rather than round
items.cause: Rolling was consistently in the same orientation without rotating the cord
items.fix: Turn the cord 90 degrees every 3 to 4 minutes of rolling. The cord compacts along whichever face meets the work surface; rotating ensures all sides are compacted equally.

items.problem: The surface fibres are still loose and fuzzy after 20 minutes
items.cause: The water temperature dropped too much or the soap concentration was too low
items.fix: Rewet in fresh hot soapy water and continue the extended polished method using the tea towel. Hotter water and firm pressure are both necessary to surface the outer fibres into the cord body.

content.text: Finished cords are durable and do not require special care in use. If a cord used as a handle or jewellery component becomes dirty, hand-wash briefly in cool water with a drop of wool wash, rinse, press between dry towels, and dry straight. Avoid prolonged soaking, which can soften the surface. Dense cords made by the extended polished method will not stretch in use; quick-method cords may elongate slightly under load and are better suited to decorative rather than structural applications.
